informal: Let $\mathcal{T}_\alpha$ be a family of topologies on $X$. Show that there is a unique largest topology on $X$ contained in all the collections $\mathcal{T}_\alpha$.
lean:
theorem Munkres_exercise_13_4b2 (X I : Type*) (T : I → Set (Set X)) (h : ∀ i, is_topology X (T i)) :
  ∃! T', is_topology X T' ∧ (∀ i, T' ⊆ T i) ∧
  ∀ T'', is_topology X T'' → (∀ i, T'' ⊆ T i) → T'' ⊆ T'
reasoning: The Lean signature asserts the existence of a unique T' that (a) is a topology on X, (b) is contained in every T i, and (c) contains every other topology T'' that is contained in all T i. This is precisely the statement that there is a unique largest topology on X contained in all the T_α. The family is indexed by an arbitrary type I (matching "family"), each T i is required to be a topology via the hypothesis h, and "largest" is correctly encoded by the maximality clause. Uniqueness via ∃! together with the maximality condition matches the informal "unique largest". No extra or missing hypotheses are introduced.
